# Special vs. Common

Status: Partial

# I Gen

Modern media—especially modern fantasy media—has increasingly relied on the message of, “You are special.”

This isn’t a new idea.  In a way, the devil appealed to Eve being special.

However, as technology has advanced and each person becomes better equipped to carve out his own personal universe, narcissistic pursuit of individualism has escalated.

# Common roles

It is easy to subversively impart a warped worldview when portraying characters with unique abilities and roles.

In the real world, if someone can do a thing, then there are other people who can do that thing as well.

If someone misses an opportunity to invent something valuable, it is only a matter of time before someone else will invent it.

## Justification

This unrealistic uniqueness is often used to excuse bad morality.

When a narrative portrays that there is only one person in the world who can save said world, then they have to pay whatever price to do so, no matter how immoral.

# Wonder

As I continue to explore wonder, I keep finding cases where wonder is tied to things being special.

When I find a fresh spot of wonder, there is specialness, standing nearby observing me knowingly.

I’m beginning to suspect that specialness may be an essential attribute of wonder.

# Narrow scope

There are many husbands in this world, and many wives.

Within the context of each marriage, there is one husband and one wife.

Within the context of  marriage, each spouse is special.

Within narrow scopes, a common instance can be unique.

# Narrow fable scope

Narrowing the scope can be a key part of evoking a sense of fable, which is related to the kind of wonder I am seeking (and in turn related to how at least some wonder seems to be dependent upon specialness.)

# Broken scope

Part of the problem I was seeing in exaggerated specialness was broken scoping.

The narrower the scope, the more special each person is.

The broader the scope, the less special each person is.

There can be real life exceptions where a single person is special within a large group of people, but that is uncommon.

When those cases do arise, they generally have clear causality as to how that unusual ratio of distinction came to be.

For example, when a single missionary visits a remote tribal island, that missionary can be unique in a variety of ways.

Those distinctions and what led to them are not random or mysterious, they are straightforward and easy to analyze.

But when a story claims that the most brilliant computer hacker in the entire world is a punk teenager, that should raise serious doubts and questions as to whether such a claim is valid.

# Batman and the battle for Gotham City

[Batman (1989)](../../Inspirations/Inspirations/Batman%20(1989)%20136bf2ccc849460a914cad03bb8dc541.md) has a fable quality to it.  (Tim Burton has said he wanted the Batman movies to be fables to some degree.)

The movie feels like Batman is the only hero in the world, and the Joker is the only villain.

This isn’t uncommon with superhero stories, but Batman+Gotham seems to generally handle that better than most other superheroes and their cities, and [Batman (1989)](../../Inspirations/Inspirations/Batman%20(1989)%20136bf2ccc849460a914cad03bb8dc541.md) is one of the best examples of that.

Within the context of that movie, the exaggerated specialness doesn’t feel arrogant because the context is so surreal.

For one, there is never any mention of a world outside Gotham City.

Gotham City feels like its own pocket universe.

Contrast that with the Dark Knight trilogy.

In the Dark Knight trilogy, Christopher Nolan tried to make the Batman mythology more grounded.

But in doing so, it becomes a harder sell to to say Batman is Gotham’s only hope.

In the trilogy’s defense, much of the plot is spent making a case for how Batman can be so amazing and the villains so unstoppable by any forces other than Batman.

<aside>
💡

As a quick aside, here’s my ranking of the Dark Knight trilogy:

1. First movie - Solid
2. Second movie - Amazing
3. Third movie - Forgettable
</aside>

# I’m not special, but he whom I serve is

> I’m not special, but my employer is - [James](../Characters/James%2014f6a0f77a694f4cb18881bb58846c94.md)
> 

# Effective unique roles

However, there are cases where unique roles don’t feel narcissistic.

## Keymaker

One of my favorite cases of a healthy and well implemented unique role is the Keymaker in the *Matrix Reloaded*.

As far as the narrative goes, the Keymaker is the only character in the universe who can perform his particular roles, yet he is such a humble servant.

His role is low key and doesn’t involve much screen time, yet he is one of the most memorable aspects of that movie (at least for me).

# Conclusion: Special responsibly

Specialness seems to be an essential ingredient for what I am pursuing, but it must only be used in the right ways and places.
